full_response:
We work with the Kruskal–Szekeres extension of the Schwarzschild space–time.  
The Kruskal coordinates (U ,V ) are related to the usual Schwarzschild
coordinates (t ,r ) by  

 U = −e^{-(t-r_{*})/4M},  V =  e^{ (t+r_{*})/4M},      
 r_{*}=r+2M\ln\!\left|\dfrac{r}{2M}-1\right| .                (1)

(Here c = G = 1 and M ≡ GM.)

1.  Implicit relation between r and (U ,V)

Multiplying the definitions (1) gives  

 UV = \bigl(1-\dfrac{r}{2M}\bigr)\,e^{r/2M}.                                (2)

Equation (2) tells us which point (U ,V ) corresponds to a given value of r.

2.  Locate the surface r = 0

Set r = 0 in (2):

 UV = (1-0)\,e^{0} = 1.                                                     (3)

Hence the singular surface r = 0 is the hyperbola  

 UV = 1.                                                                    (4)

3.  Sign of U and V inside the horizon

Inside the horizon (r < 2M) the right-hand side of (2) is positive
(1 − r/2M > 0), so UV > 0 there.  
Consequently, inside the horizon one must have either

 U > 0 , V > 0  (region II, the black-hole interior),                      (5a)  
or  
 U < 0 , V < 0  (region IV, the white-hole interior).                      (5b)

4.  Two disconnected pieces of r = 0

Equation (4) together with (5) yields two distinct branches of the
singular surface:

•  UV = 1 with U > 0 , V > 0 –– a spacelike line lying to the future of every world-line in region II.  
•  UV = 1 with U < 0 , V < 0 –– a spacelike line lying to the past of every world-line in region IV.

Because region II represents the black-hole interior, the first branch
is the usual future singularity one inevitably hits after crossing the
event horizon.  
Region IV, on the other hand, is the time-reversed (white-hole) interior;
world-lines there emerge from r = 0, so the second branch is a past
singularity.

Thus the single equation r(U ,V ) = 0 actually describes two separate,
non-intersecting singular surfaces in the maximally extended
Schwarzschild space–time: one in the future of region II and one in the
past of region IV.

\boxed{\,r(U,V)=0 \Longleftrightarrow UV=1,\; \text{giving }
  \begin{cases}
      U>0,\;V>0 &\text{(future singularity of region II)},\\
      U<0,\;V<0 &\text{(past singularity of region IV)}.
  \end{cases}} 

Confidence: 77%
